candidates.direction_name: Restore The Clean Compact PTX Wait-Sync Anchor After The Partial X32 Recovery
ranking_notes: This is top-ranked because the source and runtime together identify a specific residual regression that blocks the branch from reaching the accepted anchor.
It has better information value than opening a new family because the expected outcome is directly tied to a known-good prior measurement.
Frontier should prefer this over the older generic restore candidates because it encodes the exact remaining gap in the current branch state.
candidates.hypothesis: The current run is not on the true accepted anchor yet. Reinstating the unconditional `__syncthreads()` after `cp_async_wait_group_0()` in the compact PTX loop should restore the clean round-14 anchor behavior and recover the missing ~0.20 ms without reopening any larger-footprint family.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: The immediate issue is a residual sync-seam regression inside the compact PTX loop, not a broader occupancy or staging family choice.
candidates.risk: Low. This is a one-site restore back to an already measured anchor behavior.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the restore would require edits outside the compact PTX loop handoff or if the source already contains the unconditional sync.
expected_local_changes: Reinsert the unconditional `__syncthreads()` immediately after `cp_async_wait_group_0()` in the compact PTX microkernel loop.
Leave the dispatch selection, grouped_rows=4 mapping, peeled row band, and tail path unchanged.

candidates.direction_name: Resume Compact Sync-Family Tuning Only After The Clean Anchor Returns
ranking_notes: This family remains the best low-footprint follow-up, but it should not outrank the one-site anchor repair.
Keeping it second preserves search continuity without conflating anchor recovery with a fresh experiment.
Any revisit should explicitly avoid replaying the already measured refill-before-wait and final-no-refill-sync variants.
candidates.hypothesis: Once the unconditional sync is restored and remeasured, the remaining headroom is still most likely in a carefully bounded compact sync-family tweak that does not increase register or shared-memory footprint.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Residual barrier overhead on the true compact PTX anchor after the seam restore.
candidates.risk: Moderate. The family targets the right hotspot, but recent tiny seam experiments have mostly flattened or regressed.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the proposal would duplicate the round-13 or round-15 compact seam variants.
expected_local_changes: Keep any follow-up confined to the compact PTX loop synchronization cadence after the clean anchor is re-established.

candidates.direction_name: Keep The Existing X32 Staged Family Closed Until Its Footprint Drops Materially
ranking_notes: This stays listed only to document why the staged family is not being chosen again immediately.
Any future reopen must be tied to a concrete plan to lower both registers and shared memory, not another like-for-like probe.
It ranks last because the branch still has an unfinished clean-anchor restore ahead of any justified staged-family revisit.
candidates.hypothesis: The existing x32 staged family should remain deprioritized because the live data already showed a decisive occupancy and synchronization loss. It only becomes viable again if a future variant reduces its resident footprint materially.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Occupancy and latency hiding would still dominate any reopen of the current staged family.
candidates.risk: High. The current staged family is already proven bad on this branch.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the proposed reopen keeps the same resident footprint class as the failed existing x32 kernel.
expected_local_changes: Do not reopen the existing x32 staged family without a concrete register/shared-memory budget reduction.

notes: Projected exactly three node_b directions into normalized search candidates.
